title: Suspected Child Physical Abuse (Non-Accidental Trauma) in a 7-Month-Old Brought for 'Fussiness' with a History Inconsistent with the Injuries: Recognizing Red Flags (Injury Inconsistent with Developmental Stage, Patterned Bruising, Abusive Head Trauma with Retinal Hemorrhages), Ensuring the Child's Safety and Medical Workup, the Nurse's MANDATED-REPORTER Duty to Report Reasonable Suspicion, Objective Documentation, and Non-Confrontational, Multidisciplinary Care
subtitle: CHILD PHYSICAL ABUSE / NON-ACCIDENTAL TRAUMA (NAT) must be recognized by nurses, who are LEGALLY MANDATED REPORTERS. RED-FLAG features that should raise suspicion: (1) HISTORY concerns — a history that is VAGUE, INCONSISTENT, or CHANGES over time or between caregivers; a mechanism that is IMPLAUSIBLE or INCONSISTENT with the type/severity of injury; an injury INCONSISTENT WITH the child's DEVELOPMENTAL STAGE (the cardinal clue — e.g., a NON-MOBILE infant who reportedly 'rolled off the couch' or 'fell' sustaining significant injury; the adage 'those who don't CRUISE rarely BRUISE' — bruising in a young, non-ambulatory infant is a major red flag); a DELAY in seeking care; blaming a sibling/pet; or a caregiver affect/response that is incongruent. (2) INJURY patterns — BRUISES in MULTIPLE STAGES of healing, or in unusual/relatively protected areas (TORSO, EARS, NECK, buttocks, genitals, and the FRENULUM); PATTERNED marks (loop/cord, hand/slap, bite, cigarette burns; IMMERSION/scald burns with sharp 'waterline' demarcation, stocking-glove distribution, or sparing of flexion creases); FRACTURES highly specific for abuse — classic METAPHYSEAL ('bucket-handle/corner') lesions, POSTERIOR RIB fractures, fractures of DIFFERENT AGES, and fractures (especially of long bones, or any fracture) in a NON-AMBULATORY infant; and ABUSIVE HEAD TRAUMA (formerly 'shaken baby syndrome') — the triad-associated picture of SUBDURAL HEMORRHAGE + RETINAL HEMORRHAGES + ENCEPHALOPATHY, frequently with NO external signs of injury, presenting nonspecifically as lethargy, irritability, poor feeding, vomiting, a bulging/full fontanelle, seizures, or apnea. WORKUP (per the medical team): a SKELETAL SURVEY (to find occult/healing fractures, indicated in young children <2 years with suspected abuse), HEAD imaging (CT/MRI), a dilated OPHTHALMOLOGY exam for RETINAL HEMORRHAGES, labs to evaluate for bleeding/clotting disorders and bone disease (to exclude MIMICS such as a coagulopathy, osteogenesis imperfecta, or metabolic bone disease — abuse should not be 'diagnosed' by exclusion bias, nor mimics ignored), and PHOTOGRAPHS of visible injuries. THE NURSE'S MANDATED-REPORTER DUTY (central): nurses (and other healthcare professionals) are LEGALLY REQUIRED to REPORT a REASONABLE SUSPICION of child abuse or neglect to CHILD PROTECTIVE SERVICES (and/or law enforcement per jurisdiction) — KEY POINTS: you report SUSPICION, NOT PROOF (you do NOT have to be certain, and you do NOT investigate, gather evidence, or 'prove' it yourself — that is CPS's/law enforcement's role); the duty is INDIVIDUAL and NON-DELEGABLE (you cannot simply assume a physician or someone else will report — make/ensure the report); reporting can be done WITHOUT parental consent and OVERRIDES confidentiality (child protection supersedes); GOOD-FAITH reports are protected from liability, while FAILURE to report a reasonable suspicion can carry legal/professional penalties; report TIMELY per policy/law. SAFETY FIRST: address the child's immediate MEDICAL needs (ABCs, treat injuries) AND ensure the child's SAFETY — the child must NOT be discharged into a potentially unsafe situation pending evaluation (CPS involvement, possible protective hold/admission). NON-CONFRONTATIONAL, NON-JUDGMENTAL APPROACH WITH THE CAREGIVER: do NOT confront, accuse, interrogate, or express anger toward the caregiver, and do not 'play detective' to extract a confession — this is unsafe (may escalate, may prompt flight with the child) and is not the nurse's role; instead obtain the history with open, non-leading questions, remain calm and professional, and keep the focus on the child's care and safety; recognize that abuse occurs across ALL socioeconomic/racial/cultural groups (avoid bias in either direction). OBJECTIVE DOCUMENTATION (critical — it may become legal evidence): document FACTUALLY and OBJECTIVELY — describe injuries precisely (exact LOCATION, SIZE, SHAPE/PATTERN, COLOR) using body diagrams and photographs per policy; record the caregiver's and (if developmentally able) the child's statements VERBATIM in quotation marks; document the timeline and who was notified/when; AVOID conclusory, judgmental, or accusatory language (e.g., do not write 'the mother abused the child' — describe observations and quotes and that a report was made for suspected abuse). MULTIDISCIPLINARY response: involve the CHILD-PROTECTION TEAM / child-abuse pediatrician, SOCIAL WORK, CPS, law enforcement and forensic evaluation as indicated, and provide trauma-informed support to the child. CONTEXT/CONTRAST: child NEGLECT (failure to provide for needs) is also reportable; and for medical neglect / parental refusal of clearly life-saving treatment, the state can intervene via court order under the best-interest/parens patriae standard (see [[case-080]]). NURSING ROLE: recognize the red flags of non-accidental trauma; ensure the child's immediate medical care AND safety; FULFILL the mandated-reporter duty by reporting reasonable suspicion to CPS (without needing proof, without delegating it away, without parental consent); document objectively and precisely; approach the caregiver non-judgmentally and without confrontation; and coordinate the multidisciplinary child-protection response

patient.name: Baby R.
patient.code: Full code; infant — parents/guardians are decision-makers, but child protection supersedes confidentiality when abuse is suspected; goal = treat the child's injuries, ensure the child's safety, fulfill mandated-reporter duty, and coordinate child-protection evaluation
patient.admitDate: Brought to the pediatric ED by a parent for 'fussiness and not acting right,' with a reported history that the infant 'rolled off the couch.' On assessment the 7-month-old is lethargic and has been vomiting, with a full/bulging anterior fontanelle; there is patterned bruising on the torso and an ear, and the history is INCONSISTENT with the injuries and with the infant's developmental stage. Findings raise strong concern for NON-ACCIDENTAL TRAUMA (including possible abusive head trauma). The nurse ensures the child's medical stabilization and safety, recognizes the red flags, fulfills the mandated-reporter duty, documents objectively, and engages the child-protection team — all while remaining non-confrontational with the caregiver.
patient.room: Pediatric ED → admission; child-protection team / child-abuse pediatrician + social work + CPS + (per jurisdiction) law enforcement engaged; non-judgmental, child-safety-focused environment

vitals.time: ED arrival — recognition of red flags
vitals.temp: 37.0°C; 7-month-old, LETHARGIC with episodes of vomiting and a FULL/BULGING anterior fontanelle (concerning for increased intracranial pressure / abusive head trauma); exam reveals BRUISING in multiple areas including the TORSO and an EAR (protected sites) and what appears to be a patterned mark; HISTORY ('rolled off the couch') is INCONSISTENT with the injuries and with a non-mobile/limited-mobility infant's developmental stage = red flags for NON-ACCIDENTAL TRAUMA

37.0°C; address ABCs + neuro status (the infant's medical needs come first); ensure the child's SAFETY (will not be discharged into a potentially unsafe situation pending evaluation); IV access + labs; the team initiates the abuse workup; nurse maintains a calm, non-confrontational, non-accusatory approach with the caregiver while focusing on the child

37.0°C; medical team orders a SKELETAL SURVEY (occult/healing fractures), HEAD imaging (CT/MRI), a dilated OPHTHALMOLOGY exam (RETINAL HEMORRHAGES), and labs to evaluate for bleeding/clotting disorders + bone disease (exclude mimics); injuries photographed + documented on a body diagram; findings include subdural hemorrhage + retinal hemorrhages + a healing posterior rib fracture (highly concerning for abusive head trauma/NAT)

37.0°C; the nurse FULFILLS the MANDATED-REPORTER duty — reports the REASONABLE SUSPICION of child abuse to Child Protective Services (and law enforcement per jurisdiction) WITHOUT needing proof, WITHOUT requiring parental consent, and WITHOUT assuming someone else will report; documentation is OBJECTIVE/factual (precise injury descriptions, caregiver quotes verbatim, who/when notified) with NO accusatory/conclusory language; child-protection team + social work engaged

37.0°C; admitted for treatment of injuries + neurologic monitoring; CHILD-PROTECTION team / child-abuse pediatrician, social work, CPS, and law enforcement coordinating the safety evaluation/placement; trauma-informed support for the child; caregiver treated non-judgmentally + professionally (no confrontation/interrogation by nursing); ongoing objective documentation

37.0°C; medically stabilizing; CPS/child-protection determine a safe disposition (the child is NOT released to an unsafe environment); continued medical treatment + follow-up arranged; the nurse's mandated report, objective documentation, and child-safety advocacy fulfilled the legal/ethical duties while the child received needed care

labs.n: PRESENTATION + recognizing non-accidental trauma red flags
labs.v: 7-month-old male, 'Baby R.,' brought to the pediatric ED by a parent for 'fussiness and not acting right,' with a stated history that the infant 'ROLLED OFF THE COUCH.' On assessment he is LETHARGIC with episodes of VOMITING and a FULL/BULGING anterior FONTANELLE (concerning for increased intracranial pressure), and the exam reveals BRUISING in multiple locations including the TORSO and an EAR (relatively protected sites where accidental bruising is unusual) and an apparent patterned mark. Critically, the HISTORY is INCONSISTENT with the injuries and with the infant's DEVELOPMENTAL STAGE — at ~7 months the mechanism described is implausible for the severity/type of injury, and significant bruising in a young, minimally-mobile infant is itself a red flag ('those who don't cruise rarely bruise'). This constellation raises STRONG SUSPICION for CHILD PHYSICAL ABUSE / NON-ACCIDENTAL TRAUMA (NAT), including possible ABUSIVE HEAD TRAUMA. RED FLAGS to recognize generally: a vague/inconsistent/changing history; a mechanism implausible or inconsistent with the injury; injury inconsistent with developmental stage; delayed care-seeking; injuries in protected areas (torso/ears/neck/buttocks/frenulum) or in multiple stages of healing; patterned injuries (loop/cord, hand, bite, immersion burns); and specific fractures (metaphyseal corner, posterior rib, different ages, fractures in non-ambulatory infants). PMH/SOCIAL: per limited history; the nurse gathers information non-judgmentally and documents objectively. The nurse's priorities, in order: (1) ensure the child's immediate MEDICAL needs (ABCs, neuro) and SAFETY; (2) recognize the red flags; (3) support the medical WORKUP; (4) fulfill the MANDATED-REPORTER duty (report reasonable suspicion); (5) DOCUMENT objectively; (6) approach the caregiver NON-CONFRONTATIONALLY; and (7) coordinate the multidisciplinary CHILD-PROTECTION response. Recognizing abuse is a critical, life-saving nursing competency — missed abuse can be fatal (a sentinel injury overlooked can precede a lethal one).
labs.r: Child physical abuse/NAT red flags: history vague/inconsistent/changing or IMPLAUSIBLE for the injury, injury INCONSISTENT WITH DEVELOPMENTAL STAGE (e.g., significant injury/bruising in a non-mobile infant), delayed care, bruises in protected areas/multiple ages, patterned injuries, specific fractures, and abusive head trauma (subdural + retinal hemorrhages + encephalopathy, often no external signs). RN role: recognize red flags + ensure medical care/safety + report + document + non-confrontational care.

labs.n: Injury patterns, abusive head trauma, and the workup (with mimics)
labs.v: SPECIFIC INJURY PATTERNS that heighten concern for abuse: BRUISING in MULTIPLE STAGES of healing (different colors) and in unusual/protected locations — TORSO, EARS, NECK, buttocks, genitalia, and a torn frenulum; PATTERNED marks reflecting an implement or hand — LOOP/CORD marks, hand/slap marks, BITE marks, cigarette burns; and BURNS suggesting inflicted immersion — a sharp 'waterline' demarcation, a stocking-glove (immersion) distribution, or sparing of flexion creases (where the child pulled away). FRACTURES that are highly specific for abuse: classic METAPHYSEAL lesions ('bucket-handle'/corner fractures), POSTERIOR RIB fractures, fractures of DIFFERENT AGES (indicating repeated injury), and ANY fracture (especially long-bone) in a NON-AMBULATORY infant. ABUSIVE HEAD TRAUMA (AHT; formerly 'shaken baby syndrome') is a leading cause of abuse fatality and is often missed because external signs may be ABSENT: it classically combines SUBDURAL HEMORRHAGE, RETINAL HEMORRHAGES, and ENCEPHALOPATHY, presenting nonspecifically as LETHARGY, irritability, poor feeding, VOMITING, a FULL/BULGING FONTANELLE, increased head circumference, SEIZURES, or APNEA (as in this infant). WORKUP (medical team): a SKELETAL SURVEY (standardized radiographic series) to detect occult/healing fractures (recommended for children <2 years with suspected abuse; sometimes repeated in ~2 weeks); HEAD CT/MRI; a DILATED OPHTHALMOLOGIC exam for RETINAL HEMORRHAGES; LABS to assess for bleeding/CLOTTING disorders and BONE disease (CBC, coagulation studies, and others) to EXCLUDE MEDICAL MIMICS — e.g., a coagulopathy/bleeding disorder (can cause easy bruising), OSTEOGENESIS IMPERFECTA or metabolic bone disease (can cause fractures), birthmarks/Mongolian spots (mistaken for bruises), and cultural practices (e.g., cupping/coining) — because the goal is ACCURATE assessment (neither missing abuse nor falsely accusing); and PHOTODOCUMENTATION of visible injuries. Importantly, evaluating for mimics does NOT delay reporting a reasonable suspicion — suspicion is reported while the workup proceeds. THIS INFANT'S findings (subdural + retinal hemorrhages + a healing posterior rib fracture, with an inconsistent history) are highly concerning for abusive head trauma/NAT. The nurse assists with/coordinates the workup, ensures injuries are photographed and documented, and recognizes the significance of these findings.
labs.r: Concerning injuries: bruises in multiple ages/protected areas (torso/ears/neck/frenulum); patterned marks (loop/hand/bite, immersion burns with sharp lines/sparing creases); fractures highly specific for abuse (metaphyseal/corner, posterior rib, different ages, any in a non-ambulatory infant); ABUSIVE HEAD TRAUMA = subdural + retinal hemorrhages + encephalopathy, often no external signs (lethargy/vomiting/bulging fontanelle/seizures/apnea). Workup: skeletal survey + head imaging + ophtho exam + labs to exclude mimics (coagulopathy, osteogenesis imperfecta) + photos. RN role: recognize + support workup + photodocument.

labs.v: Nurses are LEGALLY MANDATED REPORTERS of suspected child abuse and neglect — this is the pivotal ethical/legal nursing responsibility in this case. KEY PRINCIPLES: (1) REPORT REASONABLE SUSPICION, NOT PROOF — the legal threshold is a reasonable suspicion or 'reason to believe' that abuse/neglect has occurred; the nurse does NOT need to be CERTAIN, and does NOT need to (and should NOT try to) INVESTIGATE, gather forensic evidence, or 'prove' the abuse — investigation is the role of CHILD PROTECTIVE SERVICES (CPS) and law enforcement. (2) The duty is INDIVIDUAL and NON-DELEGABLE — a mandated reporter who personally has the suspicion is obligated to ensure a report is made and CANNOT simply assume that the physician, charge nurse, or someone else will do it (in many jurisdictions each mandated reporter is individually responsible); follow facility policy (which often involves notifying the provider/social work AND ensuring the report to CPS is filed), but the suspicion must be reported. (3) Reporting does NOT require PARENTAL CONSENT and OVERRIDES confidentiality/HIPAA — protecting the child takes precedence, and disclosure to CPS/authorities is legally permitted/required. (4) GOOD-FAITH reporting is PROTECTED from civil/criminal liability (immunity) even if abuse is ultimately not substantiated, whereas FAILURE TO REPORT a reasonable suspicion can result in legal penalties and professional discipline (and, worse, a child returned to danger). (5) Report TIMELY (immediately/within the legally specified window) to the designated agency (CPS hotline and/or law enforcement, per state law), and document the report. The nurse does NOT confront or accuse the caregiver as a prerequisite to reporting, does NOT need to tell the caregiver who reported, and does NOT delay reporting to 'be sure' or to await all test results. THIS NURSE recognized the reasonable suspicion (inconsistent history + concerning injuries + AHT findings) and ENSURED a report was made to CPS (and law enforcement per jurisdiction) without proof, without parental consent, and without assuming someone else would do it — fulfilling the mandated-reporter duty.
labs.r: Mandated reporter: REPORT reasonable SUSPICION (not proof) of child abuse/neglect to CPS (± law enforcement); do NOT investigate/prove it yourself (that's CPS's role). Duty is INDIVIDUAL + NON-DELEGABLE (don't assume someone else reports). No parental consent needed; OVERRIDES confidentiality. Good-faith reporting is immune from liability; failure to report = legal/professional penalties. Report TIMELY + document. RN role: ensure the report is made on reasonable suspicion.

labs.n: Child safety, non-confrontational approach, and objective documentation
labs.v: CHILD SAFETY + immediate MEDICAL care come first: stabilize and treat the child's injuries (ABCs, neuro monitoring for AHT/increased ICP), and ensure the child is NOT discharged into a potentially unsafe situation while the evaluation proceeds — this may mean ADMISSION and/or a protective hold with CPS/law-enforcement involvement; the nurse advocates for the child's safety throughout. NON-CONFRONTATIONAL, NON-JUDGMENTAL APPROACH WITH THE CAREGIVER: the nurse must NOT confront, accuse, interrogate, or express anger or suspicion toward the caregiver, and must NOT try to 'play detective' to elicit a confession — doing so is NOT the nurse's role and is counterproductive and potentially dangerous (it can escalate the situation, damage the ability to help the child, or prompt the caregiver to flee with the child); instead, obtain the history using OPEN, NON-LEADING questions, remain calm and professional, keep the focus on the child's care, and recognize that abuse occurs across ALL socioeconomic, racial, and cultural groups (guard against both under- and over-suspicion based on bias). OBJECTIVE, FACTUAL DOCUMENTATION is essential because the record may become LEGAL EVIDENCE: describe each injury PRECISELY — exact LOCATION, SIZE (measured), SHAPE/PATTERN, and COLOR — using BODY DIAGRAMS and PHOTOGRAPHS per facility policy; record the caregiver's statements (and any developmentally-appropriate statements from the child) VERBATIM in QUOTATION MARKS; document the timeline, the child's behavior/affect, and exactly WHO was notified and WHEN; and AVOID conclusory, judgmental, or accusatory language — do NOT write opinions like 'the parent abused the child'; instead document the objective findings, the quotes, and the fact that a report was made for SUSPECTED abuse. MULTIDISCIPLINARY: engage the CHILD-PROTECTION team / child-abuse pediatrician, SOCIAL WORK, CPS, and law enforcement/forensic evaluation as indicated; provide trauma-informed, developmentally appropriate support to the child; and support siblings who may also be at risk (CPS assesses the household). The nurse's calm, child-focused, objective stance both protects the child and preserves the integrity of the process. THIS NURSE prioritized the infant's medical stabilization and safety, remained non-confrontational with the caregiver, documented objectively with precise injury descriptions and verbatim quotes, and coordinated the child-protection team.
labs.r: Safety + medical care FIRST — treat injuries + do NOT discharge into an unsafe situation (admit/protective hold + CPS). Approach the caregiver NON-confrontationally/NON-judgmentally (don't accuse/interrogate/play detective — it's unsafe + not the nurse's role; abuse crosses all groups). DOCUMENT objectively (precise injury location/size/pattern/color + body diagram/photos + caregiver/child quotes verbatim + who/when notified; NO conclusory/accusatory language). Engage child-protection team/social work/CPS/forensics; assess siblings. RN role: child safety + non-confrontation + objective documentation + coordination.

labs.v: PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): the nurse did NOT need PROOF to report — a reasonable SUSPICION suffices; did NOT try to INVESTIGATE/prove the abuse or extract a confession (that is CPS/law enforcement's role); did NOT ASSUME the physician or someone else would report (the duty is individual/non-delegable) and ensured the report was made; did NOT require PARENTAL CONSENT to report and recognized that reporting OVERRIDES confidentiality; did NOT delay reporting pending all test results or certainty; did NOT CONFRONT/accuse/interrogate the caregiver or show anger; did NOT discharge the child into a potentially unsafe situation; did NOT document conclusory/judgmental opinions; and did NOT let bias (about the family's demographics) cause under- or over-suspicion. The team also evaluated for MEDICAL MIMICS (coagulopathy, osteogenesis imperfecta, cultural practices/Mongolian spots) so as not to falsely accuse — while still reporting the reasonable suspicion. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: prompt RECOGNITION of the red flags (inconsistent history + injuries inconsistent with developmental stage + abusive-head-trauma findings); immediate medical stabilization + child SAFETY (no unsafe discharge); timely MANDATED REPORT to CPS; thorough, OBJECTIVE documentation (precise descriptions, photos, verbatim quotes, notification timeline) that supports the child's protection; a calm, non-confrontational caregiver approach that kept the situation safe; and a coordinated MULTIDISCIPLINARY child-protection response (child-abuse pediatrician, social work, CPS, law enforcement) including assessment of siblings. CONTEXT/CONTRAST: this is PHYSICAL abuse; child NEGLECT (failure to meet a child's basic needs) is ALSO reportable; and for MEDICAL neglect / parental refusal of clearly LIFE-SAVING treatment for a child, the state can intervene via COURT ORDER under the best-interest/parens patriae standard (parallel to overriding parental refusal — see [[case-080]]; and contrast with a competent ADULT's own refusal in [[case-122]]). EXPECTED outcome: the child receives needed medical care, is protected from further harm (safe disposition determined by CPS/the team), and the nurse fulfills the legal/ethical mandated-reporter and advocacy duties. NURSING ROLE: recognize NAT; stabilize + protect the child; report reasonable suspicion (without proof/consent, non-delegable, overriding confidentiality); document objectively; approach the caregiver non-judgmentally without confrontation; and coordinate the multidisciplinary child-protection response — the child's safety is the paramount concern.
labs.r: Negatives confirm correct practice: report SUSPICION not proof; don't investigate/confront/accuse or delegate the report away or wait for certainty/consent; don't discharge into danger; document objectively (no conclusory language); evaluate mimics but still report; avoid bias. Protective: recognition + stabilization/safety + timely mandated report + objective documentation + non-confrontational approach + multidisciplinary child protection (+ sibling assessment). Context: neglect also reportable; medical neglect → court order ([[case-080]]); contrast competent-adult refusal ([[case-122]]). RN role: recognize + protect + report + document + coordinate.

nursesNote: Pediatric ED — 7-month-old male 'Baby R.' brought by a parent for 'fussiness/not acting right'; reported history 'rolled off the couch.' EXAM: LETHARGIC + vomiting + FULL/BULGING anterior fontanelle (concern for increased ICP / abusive head trauma); BRUISING on the TORSO + an EAR (protected sites) + an apparent patterned mark; HISTORY INCONSISTENT with the injuries + with the infant's developmental stage (significant injury/bruising in a minimally-mobile 7-month-old — 'don't cruise rarely bruise') = RED FLAGS for NON-ACCIDENTAL TRAUMA. VS HR 150, RR 40, SpO2 97%, T 37.0 (infant). PRIORITIES: (1) MEDICAL stabilization + SAFETY first — ABCs + neuro monitoring; ensure the child is NOT discharged into a potentially unsafe situation pending evaluation; IV access + labs. (2) Recognized red flags. (3) Supported WORKUP — medical team ordered SKELETAL SURVEY (occult/healing fractures), HEAD CT/MRI, dilated OPHTHALMOLOGY exam (retinal hemorrhages), labs for bleeding/clotting + bone disease to EXCLUDE MIMICS (coagulopathy, osteogenesis imperfecta), injuries PHOTOGRAPHED + documented on a body diagram; findings = subdural hemorrhage + retinal hemorrhages + healing posterior rib fracture → highly concerning for abusive head trauma/NAT. (4) MANDATED REPORTER duty FULFILLED — reported REASONABLE SUSPICION to CPS (+ law enforcement per jurisdiction) WITHOUT needing proof, WITHOUT parental consent (overrides confidentiality), WITHOUT assuming someone else would report (individual/non-delegable duty), WITHOUT delaying for certainty/all results; report documented. (5) OBJECTIVE DOCUMENTATION — precise injury descriptions (location/size/shape-pattern/color) + body diagram/photos + caregiver and child statements VERBATIM in quotes + who/when notified; NO accusatory/conclusory language ('did not write opinions'). (6) NON-CONFRONTATIONAL caregiver approach — calm/professional, open non-leading questions; did NOT accuse/interrogate/play detective or show anger (unsafe + not the nurse's role; abuse crosses all demographics — avoid bias). (7) MULTIDISCIPLINARY — child-protection team/child-abuse pediatrician + social work + CPS + law enforcement coordinating safety evaluation/placement; trauma-informed support for the child; siblings/household assessed by CPS. DISPOSITION: admitted for injury treatment + neuro monitoring; CPS/team determine a SAFE disposition (child NOT released to an unsafe environment); follow-up arranged. CONTEXT: neglect is also reportable; medical neglect/refusal of life-saving treatment for a child → court order (best-interest/parens patriae, parallel [[case-080]]); contrast a competent ADULT's own refusal ([[case-122]]). Child safety paramount.
providerOrders: TRIAGE/ADMIT: pediatric ED → admission; 7-month-old with injuries concerning for non-accidental trauma (possible abusive head trauma)
DIAGNOSIS: suspected child physical abuse / non-accidental trauma; possible abusive head trauma; ensure medical care + child safety + mandated report
CODE STATUS: full code; child protection supersedes confidentiality where abuse is suspected
IMMEDIATE MEDICAL CARE + SAFETY:
Stabilize ABCs; neuro assessment/monitoring (lethargy, vomiting, bulging fontanelle → increased ICP/AHT); treat injuries; IV access; monitor
Ensure the child's SAFETY — do NOT discharge into a potentially unsafe situation pending evaluation (admit and/or protective hold with CPS/law-enforcement involvement)
WORKUP (per medical team):
Skeletal survey (occult/healing fractures; <2 years suspected abuse; consider repeat in ~2 weeks)
Head imaging (CT/MRI); dilated ophthalmology exam for retinal hemorrhages
Labs to evaluate for bleeding/clotting disorders and bone disease (exclude mimics: coagulopathy, osteogenesis imperfecta, metabolic) — but do NOT delay reporting for results
Photograph injuries; document on a body diagram per policy
MANDATED REPORTING (nursing/legal duty):
Report a REASONABLE SUSPICION (not proof) of child abuse/neglect to Child Protective Services (and law enforcement per jurisdiction) — timely, per policy/law

Do NOT investigate/gather evidence/prove abuse yourself (CPS/law enforcement role); do NOT assume someone else will report (individual, non-delegable duty); parental consent NOT required; reporting overrides confidentiality
Document the report (agency, time, person notified)
CAREGIVER APPROACH:

Remain calm, professional, non-judgmental, and NON-CONFRONTATIONAL; obtain history with open, non-leading questions; do NOT accuse, interrogate, or 'play detective'/seek a confession; avoid bias (abuse occurs across all groups)
OBJECTIVE DOCUMENTATION (may become legal evidence):

Describe injuries precisely (location, size, shape/pattern, color) with body diagrams + photographs; record caregiver and child statements VERBATIM in quotation marks; document timeline + who/when notified
Avoid conclusory/judgmental/accusatory language; document objective findings + quotes + that a report was made for suspected abuse
MULTIDISCIPLINARY + SUPPORT:
Engage the child-protection team / child-abuse pediatrician, social work, CPS, and law enforcement/forensic evaluation as indicated
Provide trauma-informed, developmentally appropriate support to the child; ensure siblings/household are assessed by CPS
DISPOSITION:
Continue medical treatment + neuro monitoring; CPS/child-protection team determine a safe disposition (not released to an unsafe environment); arrange follow-up

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What raises concern for non-accidental trauma?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically and legally significant.

opts.text: A history inconsistent with the injuries and inconsistent with the infant's developmental stage (a minimally-mobile 7-month-old 'rolling off the couch' with significant injury) — a cardinal red flag for abuse
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — history inconsistent with injury/development is a hallmark of NAT. RN role: recognize the red flag.

opts.text: Bruising in protected/unusual areas (torso, ear) and patterned marks — concerning for inflicted injury
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — protected-site/patterned bruising suggests abuse. RN role: recognize concerning injury patterns.

opts.text: Lethargy, vomiting, and a full/bulging fontanelle — concerning for increased ICP / abusive head trauma (which often has no external signs)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — these signal possible abusive head trauma, a leading abuse fatality. RN role: recognize + ensure neuro assessment.

opts.text: Ensure the child's immediate medical care (ABCs/neuro) AND safety — the child must not be discharged into a potentially unsafe situation pending evaluation
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — medical care + child safety are paramount. RN role: stabilize + protect.

opts.text: The nurse is a mandated reporter and must report a reasonable suspicion (not proof) of abuse to CPS
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the central legal/ethical duty. RN role: report reasonable suspicion.

opts.text: Document objectively (precise injury descriptions, body diagram/photos, verbatim quotes, notification timeline) without conclusory/accusatory language
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — objective documentation may become legal evidence. RN role: factual, precise documentation.

opts.text: Approach the caregiver non-judgmentally and non-confrontationally; engage the multidisciplinary child-protection team
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — non-confrontation + multidisciplinary coordination protect the child + process. RN role: non-confrontational, coordinated care.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document and proceed
opts.rat: Allergy documentation; routine. Not the central cue.

opts.text: Wait until the abuse is definitively proven and all imaging/labs return before reporting, so as not to falsely accuse the family
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Mandated reporters report a reasonable SUSPICION, not proof — waiting for certainty/results delays protection and may leave the child in danger. Report the suspicion now; CPS investigates.

opts.text: Confront the parent directly, accuse them of hurting the child, and interrogate them until they admit what happened
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + unsafe. Confronting/accusing/interrogating is not the nurse's role, can escalate the situation or prompt flight with the child, and can compromise the investigation. Remain non-confrontational; report to CPS, who investigates.

opts.text: Injury inconsistent with the stated mechanism or the child's developmental stage, injuries in protected areas/multiple healing stages, patterned marks, and specific fractures (metaphyseal, posterior rib, different ages) raise concern for abuse
opts.rat: CORRECT — the red-flag framework. Foundation for recognition.

opts.text: Abusive head trauma classically combines subdural hemorrhage, retinal hemorrhages, and encephalopathy and may present without external injury (lethargy, vomiting, bulging fontanelle, seizures, apnea)
opts.rat: CORRECT — AHT principle. Foundation for recognizing a leading abuse fatality.

opts.text: Nurses are mandated reporters who must report a reasonable suspicion (not proof) to CPS; the duty is individual/non-delegable, requires no parental consent, and overrides confidentiality
opts.rat: CORRECT — the mandated-reporter principle. Foundation for the legal/ethical duty.

opts.text: Good-faith reporting is protected from liability; the nurse does not investigate or prove abuse (CPS/law enforcement do)
opts.rat: CORRECT — immunity + role-clarity principle. Foundation for reporting without fear or over-reach.

opts.text: The child's safety and medical care come first; the child should not be discharged into a potentially unsafe situation; documentation must be objective and may become legal evidence
opts.rat: CORRECT — safety + objective-documentation principle. Foundation for protection.

opts.text: Evaluating for medical mimics (coagulopathy, osteogenesis imperfecta, cultural practices) is appropriate, but it does not delay reporting a reasonable suspicion
opts.rat: CORRECT — assess mimics without delaying the report. Foundation for accurate yet timely action.

opts.text: A nurse should only report abuse if they are certain it occurred and have gathered evidence to prove it
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. The threshold is reasonable SUSPICION, not certainty/proof; the nurse does not gather evidence/investigate (CPS does). Requiring proof would dangerously delay protection.

opts.text: Because the family is well-dressed and affluent (or, conversely, poor), abuse is unlikely (or likely), so suspicion can be adjusted by demographics
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Abuse occurs across ALL socioeconomic, racial, and cultural groups; basing suspicion on demographics is biased and leads to both missed abuse and false accusation. Assess objectively.

opts.text: Immediate medical stabilization + child SAFETY — treat injuries/ABCs/neuro and ensure the child is not discharged into a potentially unsafe situation
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. The child's life/medical needs + physical safety come first. RN role: stabilize + protect.

opts.text: Fulfill the mandated-reporter duty — report the reasonable suspicion to CPS (without proof/consent; do not delegate it away)
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Timely reporting triggers protection/investigation. RN role: report reasonable suspicion.

opts.text: Support the medical workup (skeletal survey, head imaging, ophthalmology, labs/mimics) and photodocument injuries
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. The workup defines injuries + excludes mimics + supports protection. RN role: support workup + photodocument.

opts.text: Document objectively (precise descriptions, body diagram/photos, verbatim quotes, notification timeline) without conclusory language
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Objective documentation may become legal evidence + supports the child. RN role: factual documentation.

opts.text: Approach the caregiver non-confrontationally and coordinate the multidisciplinary child-protection team; support the child + assess siblings
opts.rat: #5 — Important + ongoing. Non-confrontation + coordination protect the child + process. RN role: non-confrontational, coordinated care.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED.

opts.text: Stabilize the infant medically (ABCs, neuro for possible abusive head trauma) and ensure the child is not discharged into a potentially unsafe situation
opts.rat: Medical care + child safety first. RN role: stabilize + protect.

opts.text: Report the reasonable suspicion of abuse to CPS (and law enforcement per jurisdiction) promptly, without needing proof or parental consent
opts.rat: Core mandated-reporter duty. RN role: report reasonable suspicion timely.

opts.text: Support the workup (skeletal survey, head imaging, dilated eye exam, labs to exclude mimics) and photograph/document injuries on a body diagram
opts.rat: Defines injuries, excludes mimics, supports protection. RN role: support workup + photodocument.

opts.text: Document objectively with precise injury descriptions and verbatim caregiver/child quotes, plus who/when notified, avoiding conclusory language
opts.rat: Objective documentation (may be legal evidence). RN role: factual documentation.

opts.text: Remain calm and non-confrontational with the caregiver, obtain history with open non-leading questions, and engage the multidisciplinary child-protection team
opts.rat: Non-confrontational, coordinated, child-focused approach. RN role: non-confrontation + coordination.

opts.text: Recognize that abuse occurs across all demographics and assess objectively without bias; evaluate for medical mimics while still reporting the suspicion
opts.rat: Bias-free assessment + mimic evaluation without delaying the report. RN role: objective, unbiased assessment.

opts.text: Wait to report until all imaging/labs return and the abuse is definitively proven, to avoid falsely accusing the family
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — report reasonable SUSPICION, not proof; waiting delays protection. CPS investigates; the nurse reports the suspicion now.

opts.text: Confront and accuse the parent and interrogate them to obtain a confession before deciding whether to report
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG/unsafe — confronting/interrogating is not the nurse's role, can escalate/prompt flight, and compromises the investigation. Report to CPS without confrontation.

opts.text: Assume the physician or social worker will file the report, so the nurse does not need to do anything further
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — the mandated-reporter duty is individual and non-delegable; the nurse must ensure a report is made, not assume someone else will.

opts.text: Discharge the infant home with the caregiver while the workup is pending to avoid upsetting the family
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG/DANGEROUS — the child must not be discharged into a potentially unsafe situation pending evaluation; ensure safety (admit/protective hold + CPS).

opts.text: Document the conclusion 'the mother physically abused this child' as the diagnosis in the chart
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — avoid conclusory/accusatory language; document objective findings + verbatim quotes + that a report was made for SUSPECTED abuse (the determination is CPS/legal).

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Address the infant's immediate medical needs (ABCs, neuro) and ensure physical safety (no unsafe discharge)
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Stabilize + protect the child. RN role: medical care + safety.

opts.text: Recognize the red flags and support the workup (skeletal survey, head imaging, eye exam, labs); photograph/document injuries
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Define injuries + exclude mimics + photodocument. RN role: support workup + documentation.

opts.text: Fulfill the mandated-reporter duty — report the reasonable suspicion to CPS (and law enforcement per jurisdiction), ensuring the report is made
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Trigger protection/investigation by reporting. RN role: report reasonable suspicion.

opts.text: Document objectively (precise descriptions, verbatim quotes, notification timeline) and engage the multidisciplinary child-protection team/social work
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Objective documentation + multidisciplinary coordination. RN role: documentation + coordination.

opts.text: Maintain a non-confrontational caregiver approach, support the child, ensure siblings are assessed, and coordinate a safe disposition with CPS
opts.rat: #5 — Ongoing. Non-confrontation + child/sibling support + safe disposition. RN role: non-confrontational, coordinated, protective care.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each action taken, classify as Appropriate or Inappropriate.

opts.text: The nurse stabilized the infant, ensured he was not discharged into a potentially unsafe situation, and supported the workup (skeletal survey, head imaging, eye exam, labs to exclude mimics)
opts.rat: Medical care + child safety + appropriate workup. Model handling.

opts.text: The nurse reported the reasonable suspicion to CPS promptly, without proof or parental consent, and ensured the report was made rather than assuming someone else would
opts.rat: Correct fulfillment of the individual, non-delegable mandated-reporter duty on reasonable suspicion. Model handling.

opts.text: Injuries were photographed and documented precisely with verbatim caregiver/child quotes and the notification timeline, avoiding conclusory language
opts.rat: Objective, legally sound documentation. Model handling.

opts.text: The nurse remained calm and non-confrontational, used open questions, and engaged the child-protection team/social work
opts.rat: Non-confrontational, coordinated, child-focused care. Model handling.

opts.text: The nurse decided to wait to report until all test results returned and the abuse was proven, to avoid a false accusation
opts.rat: ⚠️ Mandated reporters report reasonable SUSPICION, not proof; waiting delays protection. Incorrect.

opts.text: The nurse confronted and accused the parent and interrogated them to get a confession
opts.rat: ⚠️ Confronting/interrogating is unsafe, not the nurse's role, and can compromise the child's safety and the investigation. Incorrect.

opts.text: Assuming the physician would handle it, the nurse made no report and took no further action
opts.rat: ⚠️ The mandated-reporter duty is individual and non-delegable; failing to ensure a report is a legal/ethical breach. Incorrect.

opts.text: The nurse documented 'the parent abused this child' as a conclusion in the medical record
opts.rat: ⚠️ Avoid conclusory/accusatory language; document objective findings + quotes + that a report was made for SUSPECTED abuse. Incorrect.

opts.text: The infant was admitted for treatment and neuro monitoring, and CPS/the child-protection team determined a safe disposition (not released to an unsafe environment), with siblings assessed
opts.rat: Safe disposition + ongoing care + sibling assessment. Model handling protecting the child + household.

opts.text: Because the family appeared affluent and 'nice,' the nurse dismissed the concern and discharged the infant without reporting
opts.rat: ⚠️ Bias by demographics + dismissing red flags + failing to report + unsafe discharge — multiple serious errors that endanger the child. Incorrect.

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Management of Care, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
